does: This paper introduces Flow-OPD, a post-training framework that applies on-policy distillation to Flow Matching text-to-image models to address multi-task alignment challenges. The method operates in two stages: first, it trains domain-specialized teacher models using single-reward GRPO fine-tuning to avoid conflicts between objectives; second, it consolidates these experts into a single student model through a Flow-based Cold-Start scheme and a three-step process combining on-policy sampling, task-routing labeling, and dense trajectory-level supervision. The authors introduce Manifold Anchor Regularization to improve the distillation process and demonstrate that Flow-OPD resolves the 'seesaw effect' where optimizing one metric degrades others, achieving improved performance across multiple objectives simultaneously.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in on-policy distillation from the large language model community, adapting techniques originally developed for language models to the visual generation domain. It extends prior Flow Matching research by addressing known limitations in multi-objective optimization, specifically the reward sparsity and gradient interference problems that arise when jointly training on heterogeneous objectives. The paper responds to limitations of naive multi-reward optimization approaches and connects to broader alignment literature concerned with steering generative models toward multiple desirable properties. It leverages GRPO (gradient-based reward policy optimization) fine-tuning as a foundation and builds upon the success of cold-start initialization strategies used in other domains.
enables: Flow-OPD opens pathways for more reliable multi-objective optimization in generative models beyond images, suggesting that on-policy distillation could become a standard post-training technique for aligning diffusion and flow-based models. The framework's ability to consolidate diverse expert objectives into a single model without performance degradation could enable practical deployment of AI systems balancing multiple user-specified constraints. Future work could extend this approach to other modalities and explore more sophisticated reward combination strategies. The method also suggests directions for understanding and mitigating gradient interference in multi-task learning more broadly, potentially informing how other large-scale generative models handle conflicting optimization objectives during fine-tuning.
